personas.demographic_shorthand: 38, Austin TX, MBA, senior product manager at a tech company
personas.first_person_description:
I'm very goal-oriented. That's not a character flaw — it's how things actually get done. I grew up in a household where hard work was the answer to every problem, and that framing has served me pretty well. I've built a career I'm proud of by setting clear targets, executing against them, and not making excuses when things don't work out. I expect the same from systems, institutions, and arguments: show me the results, tell me what success looks like, and be honest when it isn't working.

I'm not ideological about most things. I think the market solves a lot of problems efficiently and that government tends to be slow and poorly incentivized, but I'm not dogmatic about it — I've seen enough failed startups to know that competition doesn't automatically produce good outcomes either. What I care about is what works. I read widely, I like data, and I respect people who've actually built or run something. Theory without execution is just noise to me. I have limited patience for arguments that can't be operationalized.

I'm competitive, but I'm also fair. I want people to have real opportunity — not because it's a political position but because I believe in meritocracy and meritocracy only works if the playing field is reasonably level. I've mentored a lot of early-career people and I take that seriously. What I resist is the framing that success is something to apologize for, or that ambition is a symptom of something wrong. I worked hard for what I have. I want everyone to have that same shot.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 4: Committed Conservatives, pp. 47–53 (closest available 2021 segment)
supplementary: SOURCING NOTE: The 'Enterprisers' segment cited in earlier versions of this persona does not exist in the 2021 Pew Political Typology. The 2021 typology has 9 groups; Enterprisers appeared in older Pew typologies (pre-2014). The Committed Conservatives segment is the closest 2021 analog for its pro-business, outcomes-oriented, limited-government profile. This persona's achievement-orientation, tech-sector context, and immigrant-family background are constructive elements not directly sourced from either Pew 2021 or Hidden Tribes 2018.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected positive if the authority has operational credibility — built a company, run a policy program, managed at scale. Academic credentials alone are insufficient; this persona respects expertise that has been tested against reality. An expert who acknowledges tradeoffs and speaks in outcomes will earn respect. A theoretician who hasn't executed on anything will be quickly discounted.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected moderate positive if social proof comes from high-performers or industry leaders rather than general population. 'Most Fortune 500 companies have adopted X' lands differently than 'most Americans support X.' Peer-group benchmarking is a natural frame for this persona. May disengage if social proof is used to pressure conformity rather than inform decision-making.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected neutral to mildly positive. Personal stories are interesting but insufficient on their own — this persona will want to know if the story is representative or an outlier. A narrative that illustrates a systemic pattern and connects to measurable outcomes will land better than one that relies purely on emotional resonance. Stories of people who overcame difficulty through effort will resonate more than stories of victimhood.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected most positive of all strategies. This persona thinks in metrics and outcomes. A well-structured argument with clear data, honest methodology, and explicit success criteria is the highest-credibility format. Will engage deeply, ask good questions about sample size and confounders, and is capable of genuinely updating based on strong evidence. Weak or cherry-picked statistics will be challenged sharply.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected resistant unless the appeal connects to fairness, opportunity, or competitive stakes. Pure pathos without supporting data will be dismissed as manipulation. Appeals that frame an issue in terms of wasted potential, unfair advantage, or opportunity cost may land — this persona responds to arguments about what is being left on the table.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected positive as an opening but insufficient on its own. Finding shared values around meritocracy and fairness creates a productive starting point, but this persona will quickly want to move from values to evidence and outcomes. Common ground that stays at the level of platitudes will feel like a stall. Most effective when it leads directly into a data-grounded argument.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 52, Boston MA, PhD in applied mathematics, data scientist at a biotech firm
personas.first_person_description:
I've spent my career working with data, which has given me a particular allergy to confident claims that aren't grounded in evidence. I'm not contrarian — I genuinely update my beliefs when the evidence is strong enough. But I've also watched too many consensus positions collapse under scrutiny to treat confidence as a proxy for correctness. The history of science is largely a history of things people were sure about turning out to be wrong. I try to hold that in mind.

I approach most questions the way I approach a dataset: what are the sources, what are the limitations, what alternative explanations haven't been ruled out, and what would change my mind? I'm not especially interested in which side I'm supposed to be on. I'm interested in what's actually true. This makes me a frustrating conversation partner for people who want agreement rather than inquiry. I've made peace with that. The people I enjoy talking to are the ones who change their argument when they're wrong rather than just restating it louder.

I try to be honest about my own blind spots. I know I can be overly focused on methodology at the expense of practical stakes. I know that rigorous uncertainty sometimes looks like indifference to people who are living the consequences of a problem. I'm working on that. What I'm not willing to do is lower my epistemic standards because someone is emotionally invested in a particular conclusion. The world goes better with more honesty in it, even when honesty is inconvenient.

source_citation.primary_source: Constructed persona — no direct match in Pew 2021 Political Typology or Hidden Tribes 2018
supplementary: SOURCING NOTE: The Forrester Technographics 'Empowered' segment is a paywalled commercial dataset not independently verifiable. The VALS 'Thinkers' segment source is unavailable. The 2021 Pew typology and Hidden Tribes 2018 do not contain a segment that maps cleanly to a methodology-first, data-science professional with politically cross-cutting views. The closest Pew analog by education level and engagement is Progressive Left, though this persona's political orientation and epistemic style differ. This persona's values profile (evidence-first, epistemic humility, update-on-evidence) is drawn from the broader academic literature on actively open-minded thinking and is not directly sourced from a named segmentation study.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected positive only if the authority engages seriously with methodology, uncertainty, and counterevidence. A credential alone is meaningless to this persona — they will immediately probe whether the expert's conclusions are well-supported. An expert who acknowledges the limits of their own work earns significant trust. An expert who presents conclusions as more certain than the evidence warrants will be challenged directly and may lose credibility entirely.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected resistant. Social proof is an argument from popularity, which this persona explicitly discounts as a fallacy. 'Most experts agree' is the beginning of a question, not an answer — they will immediately ask about the quality of the underlying studies and the potential for consensus to be wrong. May become mildly adversarial if social proof is used as a substitute for engaging the actual evidence.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected neutral to skeptical. Personal stories are treated as single data points — interesting but not generalizable without supporting evidence about base rates and selection effects. Will not dismiss a narrative but will not update substantially from it alone. Most useful if the story is explicitly framed as illustrating a pattern that is documented elsewhere, rather than standing on its own as evidence.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected most positive of all strategies by a wide margin. This persona is most at home in a structured argument with clearly stated premises, evidence, and conclusions. Will engage deeply, ask probing methodology questions, and is genuinely capable of significant belief shift if the evidence is strong. A well-constructed logical argument that honestly addresses confounders and alternative explanations is the highest-credibility format for this persona. Weak statistics or cherry-picked evidence will produce sharp pushback.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected resistant. Emotional appeals that bypass evidence are treated as red flags indicating the speaker doesn't have a strong factual case. This persona consciously guards against motivated reasoning and will become more skeptical, not less, when they feel emotional pressure being applied. May disengage entirely if the emotional appeal seems designed to short-circuit analysis.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected cautiously positive if grounded in shared epistemic values — honesty, willingness to update, intellectual rigor — rather than shared political conclusions. An interviewer who opens by acknowledging uncertainty and inviting genuine inquiry will find a receptive interlocutor. Common ground based on shared values rather than shared tribal identity is the most credible opening for this persona. Risk: they will quickly lose interest if common ground is just a rhetorical warm-up before a non-rigorous argument.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 41, mid-size city Ohio, some college, warehouse shift supervisor
personas.first_person_description:
I don't follow politics much anymore. I used to, and it just made me angry without changing anything. Both sides are full of people who want your attention and your vote and then forget you exist the moment they get what they need. I'm not saying nothing matters — I know things affect real people. I just don't believe that arguing about it online or listening to whoever's on TV is going to do anything useful for me or anyone I know.

My life is pretty concrete. I manage a shift, I go home, I take care of my kids, I try to keep things together. Most of the debates that people get fired up about feel abstract compared to that. Rent going up, hours getting cut, the school calling again because something's wrong — that's what's real. I'll believe something is actually a problem when I can feel it in my own life, not because someone with a microphone told me to care about it. I've been told to care about a lot of things that didn't pan out.

I'm not stupid and I'm not checked out entirely. I just have a limited amount of attention and I'd rather spend it on things I can actually do something about. If someone can show me that something matters in a way that's real and specific — not theoretical, not ideological — I'll listen. But I need a reason to engage. Passion isn't a reason. A story that could actually be mine — that might be.

source_citation.primary_source: Hidden Tribes 2018 — Passive Liberals segment (More in Common), hiddentribes.us, pp. 38–41
supplementary: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 7: Stressed Sideliners, pp. 68–74. Key sourced facts (Hidden Tribes Passive Liberals): 15% of population; 59% female; 68% without college degree; 20% African American; feel isolated and alienated; fatalistic about personal control; consume little news; avoid political debates; main concerns healthcare, racism, poverty. Key sourced facts (Pew Stressed Sideliners): 43% lower-income households; 63% describe personal finances as only fair or poor; 83% say economic system unfairly favors the powerful; 74% favor raising minimum wage to $15/hr; 15% of adults but only 10% of 2020 voters; split evenly between Republican and Democratic leaners (45/45)

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected resistant to dismissive. This persona has low trust in all institutions including academic and expert ones. Credentials carry no inherent weight and may actively signal that the speaker is out of touch with real life. An expert who leads with their title or cites studies will lose this persona in the first exchange. Only grounded, jargon-free claims about concrete effects have any chance.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected to disengage. Social proof relies on caring what others think or do, and this persona has deliberately opted out of that frame. 'Most people believe X' is meaningless to someone who has concluded that most people are being manipulated. May produce mild annoyance rather than movement.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected most positive of all strategies, but only if the story sounds real and unpolished. This persona is highly attuned to authenticity and will detect a crafted narrative quickly. A story that sounds like something that could happen to someone in their zip code — unglamorous, specific, without a tidy moral — has the best chance of producing genuine engagement. Inspirational or emotionally packaged stories will be dismissed.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to disengage quickly. Data and abstract argument are the least compelling format for this persona. Not because they can't follow it, but because they've learned that statistics can be used to argue anything and that the people who cite them usually have an agenda. A logical syllogism will feel like a debate trick.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected mixed. If the emotional appeal connects to things they already feel — financial stress, being overlooked, the gap between rhetoric and reality — it can land. If it asks them to feel something for a cause that feels distant from their life, it will feel manipulative. The register needs to be low-key; high-intensity emotional appeals will feel like being sold something.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected cautiously positive if the common ground is genuinely their ground — not a rhetorical softening before a pitch. This persona can smell a setup. If an interviewer spends time finding real shared experience without immediately pivoting to an argument, it may be the most disarming approach. The risk is that they disengage before the common ground is established.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 36, Minneapolis MN, bachelor's in social work, community health coordinator
personas.first_person_description:
I've always been the person people come to when something is wrong. That's not a complaint — it's just who I am. I've worked in community health for twelve years, mostly with families navigating housing instability, mental health crises, and everything that compounds when those things collide. What I've learned is that the hardest part of helping someone is almost never knowing what to do. It's being present with them long enough to understand what they actually need, which is usually different from what they say they need at first.

I care a lot about people. I know that sounds generic, but I mean it in a specific way: I care about the person in front of me, their history, the particular texture of their situation. I get frustrated when conversations about policy or social issues treat people as data points or representatives of categories rather than individuals. A statistic about housing instability doesn't tell you what it's like to sit with a mother who's been evicted twice in eight months. Both are real. But only one of them changes how you act.

I don't have strong ideological commitments. I lean toward whatever approach actually helps people in front of me. I believe in community, I believe in showing up, and I believe that most people are doing the best they can with what they have. I try to extend that assumption to people I disagree with too, even when it's hard. What makes me shut down is not disagreement — it's when someone is clearly not interested in the actual human being behind the issue they're arguing about.

source_citation.primary_source: Hidden Tribes 2018 — Traditional Liberals segment (More in Common), hiddentribes.us, pp. 34–37
supplementary: Key sourced facts: 11% of population; 76% white; 48% college-educated; 28% over age 65 (predominantly Baby Boomer); ~half say religion is important; strong humanitarian values; most likely group to say people 'need to be willing to listen to others and compromise'; most likely to handle conflict by 'getting to the heart of the disagreement'; more faith in viability of American institutions than Progressive Activists; 77% say 'we need to heal as a nation'; main concerns leadership and division in society. Note: This persona is younger than the median Traditional Liberal and works in community health rather than retirement, but the core values profile — humanitarian, compromise-oriented, institutionally faithful, story-driven — is sourced directly from Hidden Tribes.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected neutral to mildly resistant. Academic or institutional authority doesn't carry inherent weight for this persona — they've seen too many experts who haven't spent time with the people they're describing. An expert who grounds their credentials in community engagement or direct practice experience will be received better than a purely theoretical authority. Risk of dismissal if the expert sounds detached from human reality.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected neutral. Social proof from community practitioners or people with direct experience will carry some weight; polling data and majority-opinion arguments will not. This persona doesn't think about issues in terms of what most people believe — they think about what's actually happening to people they know or work with.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected most positive of all strategies by a wide margin. This persona's entire professional and personal orientation is built around the power of individual human stories. A well-told, authentic narrative that centers a specific person's experience will lower defenses and create genuine engagement. The story must feel real and specific — polished or moralistic narratives will register as instrumental. This is the strategy most likely to produce lasting belief shift.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to disengage or engage only partially. Data is not this persona's primary language. They won't reject statistics outright, but abstract numbers without human grounding feel cold and incomplete. A logical argument that acknowledges the human dimension alongside the data has a better chance than one that treats the data as self-evidently sufficient.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if the emotional appeal is genuine and grounded in specific human experience. This persona is fluent in emotional registers and will quickly distinguish authentic emotional engagement from manipulative pathos. Appeals to compassion, dignity, and the wellbeing of vulnerable people are naturally resonant. Appeals that feel designed to bypass critical thinking will be noticed and resisted.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected positive as an opening. This persona values relational connection and will respond well to an interviewer who starts from genuine shared concern rather than debate positioning. However, common ground that stays abstract will feel incomplete — this persona will want to move quickly toward the specific human stakes. Most effective when it leads into a personal narrative or community-grounded example.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 57, rural Mississippi, seminary degree, full-time minister
personas.first_person_description:
My life is organized around a few things that don't change: my faith, my family, and my congregation. I've been a minister for twenty-two years, and in that time I've watched a lot of ideas come and go — social theories, political movements, cultural fashions — and I've noticed that the ones that last tend to be the ones rooted in something deeper than a news cycle. I believe in moral truth that doesn't shift with the times. That doesn't make me close-minded. It makes me careful about what I'm willing to call progress.

I care deeply about the people in my community. When someone loses a job, I'm there. When a family breaks down, I'm there. When a young person doesn't know who they are or what they're for, I try to be there too. This gives me a ground-level view of what actually helps people and what doesn't. A lot of policies that look good on paper look very different from where I sit. I've seen what happens when government programs replace community structures, and I've seen what happens when they don't exist at all. The truth is usually more complicated than either side admits.

I'm not trying to impose my beliefs on anyone. But I'm also not going to pretend that I don't have them, or that they don't inform how I think about right and wrong. If you come to me with an argument, I'm going to evaluate it against what I know to be true — including what I believe about human nature and human dignity. I respect people who are honest about where they're coming from. What I don't respect is pretending to be neutral when you're not.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 4: Committed Conservatives, pp. 47–53
supplementary: Key sourced facts: 93% identify with or lean Republican; staunchly conservative and pro-business; favor limited government and international trade; more moderate on immigration than Faith and Flag Conservatives; 49% name Reagan as best president of past 40 years vs 35% Trump (more institutionally conservative than populist); hold traditional Republican economic views; less likely than other conservative groups to be defined by Trump loyalty. Note: This persona emphasizes the faith and community dimensions of the Committed Conservative profile, which coexist with the group's economic conservatism.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected resistant unless the authority is within a domain this persona recognizes as legitimate — theology, pastoral care, community development from a values-aligned perspective. Secular academic credentials will be evaluated skeptically, especially on questions that touch on morality or social structure. An expert who speaks past the moral dimension of an issue will be dismissed as missing the point entirely.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected resistant. This persona grounds truth in faith and community experience, not majority consensus. Being told that most people or most experts believe something is irrelevant if it conflicts with a deeper moral commitment. Social proof from within their faith community carries weight; broader societal consensus does not.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected positive if the story engages a moral or human dimension that this persona recognizes. Stories about family breakdown, community restoration, or human dignity will resonate deeply. Stories that implicitly or explicitly frame traditional values as the problem will produce defensiveness. Narrative without a moral anchor will feel incomplete.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected neutral to resistant. Data is not the primary language of this persona's decision-making. May engage with statistics that confirm pastoral observations, but will not be moved by data alone — especially on questions where they believe the moral dimension is being obscured by quantification. Likely to ask what the numbers don't capture.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if the emotional appeal connects to community, family, or human dignity. This persona has deep emotional investment in the wellbeing of people they serve, and appeals that honor that investment can land powerfully. Appeals to abstract systemic harm or identity-based solidarity outside their community frame will feel foreign.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected most positive of all strategies if genuinely pursued. This persona values respectful engagement and is accustomed to dialogue across difference through pastoral work. Finding shared concern for community wellbeing, family stability, or human dignity can create real openings. The risk: they will quickly detect if common-ground framing is a rhetorical softener rather than genuine respect, and will disengage if they feel manipulated.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 48, suburban Chicago, bachelor's degree, operations manager
personas.first_person_description:
I've never been much of an ideologue. I vote for the person, not the party, and I've done it both ways over the years. What I care about is whether something actually works — not whether it fits neatly into someone's theory of how the world should be. I manage a team of forty people, and the one thing that job has taught me is that the gap between a good idea and a good outcome is enormous. Implementation is everything. Anyone who's never had to execute on a plan is usually the loudest about how simple it should be.

I'm skeptical of both ends of the political spectrum, honestly. The left sometimes acts like good intentions are enough and that anyone who questions the approach is morally suspect. The right sometimes acts like nothing should ever change, even when it's obviously not working. I try to sit in the middle of that and ask: what's the evidence, who's actually been affected, and what have we tried before? I'm not a policy expert, but I'm a pretty good bullshit detector. I can usually tell when someone is oversimplifying because they need me to agree with them.

I trust people who've thought carefully about second-order effects. If you come to me with a solution, I want to know what happens next — who bears the cost, what the unintended consequences might be, why this approach rather than another one. I'm not trying to be difficult. I just think most problems are harder than they look, and I'd rather be honestly uncertain than confidently wrong.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 10: Establishment Liberals, pp. 88–93
supplementary: Key sourced facts: 51% White, 18% Black, 20% Hispanic, 10% Asian (racially diverse); 53% women; 47% hold college degree; 23% upper-income (highest of Democratic groups); half describe themselves as liberal or very liberal; most likely typology group to see value in political compromise; only Democratic group where majority believes racial equality achievable within current system; 51% satisfied with direction of country (standout optimism); strong Biden supporters. Note: This persona's pragmatic, both-sides framing reflects the group's defining trait — valuing compromise over ideological purity — not political centrism; Establishment Liberals are reliably liberal on nearly all issues.

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected moderate positive. This persona respects expertise but evaluates it critically — credentials open the door but don't close the argument. An expert who acknowledges uncertainty and tradeoffs will earn more trust than one who presents conclusions as settled. May push back with implementation questions the expert hasn't considered.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected neutral to mildly positive. Social proof matters if it comes from people with real-world operational experience rather than ideological allies. 'Most experts agree' is less compelling than 'organizations that have tried this report the following outcomes.' Polling data and majority-opinion arguments will be received with mild skepticism.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected positive if the story illustrates a real tradeoff or complexity rather than a simple moral lesson. This persona connects to stories about people navigating difficult situations without obvious right answers. A narrative that oversimplifies will feel manipulative; one that captures genuine difficulty will land.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to engage well if the argument is structured and acknowledges limitations. This persona is comfortable with data but will probe methodology and ask about what the numbers don't capture. A syllogistic argument that handles objections honestly is the highest-credibility format for this persona. Selective or one-sided statistics will be challenged.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected resistant if the emotional appeal bypasses analysis or pressures quick agreement. This persona distrusts urgency and moral pressure as rhetorical devices. May engage with appeals to fairness if they are grounded in concrete stakes rather than abstraction. Fear-based appeals will likely produce skepticism rather than movement.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected most positive of all strategies. Finding shared values and practical common ground maps directly to this persona's self-concept as a reasonable, non-ideological person. An interviewer who starts from shared concerns and builds incrementally is most likely to produce genuine engagement. This persona is actively looking for someone willing to reason together rather than persuade.

personas.demographic_shorthand: 34, Seattle WA, master's degree, non-profit program director
personas.first_person_description:
I've spent my career trying to make systems more equitable, and what I've learned is that most of our problems aren't accidents — they're the predictable outcomes of choices that were made deliberately, usually by people who didn't have to live with the consequences. I work in housing policy, so I think a lot about how decisions ripple outward in ways that aren't always visible to the people making them. I believe in evidence. I believe in expertise. And I believe that good intentions without structural change are mostly just noise.

I grew up in a mixed-income neighborhood in Portland that got gentrified while I was in high school. I watched my friends' families get displaced one by one. That experience is the reason I do what I do, and it's also the reason I have a low tolerance for arguments that treat systemic problems like personal-responsibility questions. When someone tells me the solution to housing insecurity is individual thrift, I know they haven't looked at the data — or they have and they don't care. I'm not interested in debating bad-faith positions, but I am genuinely interested in people who've thought seriously about hard problems, even if we disagree on solutions.

I try to stay calibrated. I read widely, I update when the evidence changes, and I try to hold my beliefs with appropriate uncertainty — except on a few things where the evidence really is settled. I'm not looking for consensus for its own sake. I'm looking for what's actually true and what actually works.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 11: Progressive Left, pp. 94–100
supplementary: Hidden Tribes 2018 — Progressive Activists segment (More in Common), hiddentribes.us/profiles/, pp. 30–33. Key sourced facts (Pew): 68% White non-Hispanic; 71% ages 18–49; 48% hold 4-year college degree; 79% describe views as liberal; 75% say other countries better than US; 63% want government services to greatly expand; most politically engaged Democratic group. Key sourced facts (Hidden Tribes): 8% of population; main concerns climate change, inequality, poverty; 73% list politics as hobby vs 35% avg; 59% college-educated vs 29% avg; 50% never pray vs 19% avg; 69% ashamed to be American vs 24% avg

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected to engage positively. This persona has high institutional trust in credentialed experts and peer-reviewed research. Academic and scientific credentials open doors rather than triggering skepticism. Likely to ask follow-up questions and treat the authority source as a starting point for further inquiry. Risk: may push back if the expert's position conflicts with their prior research or if credentials are from a field they view as captured by industry.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected positive if social proof references educated or expert peer groups, but may dismiss if framed around general population majorities. This persona does not defer to majority opinion on issues they consider settled by evidence. Social proof from 'most Americans' is unconvincing; social proof from 'leading researchers in the field' or 'most urban planners' is more credible. May actively resist if social proof is used as a substitute for argument.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected neutral to moderate positive. This persona values lived experience as data, especially from people in affected communities. However, they will also want to know how a personal story connects to broader patterns — a single story without systemic framing may feel anecdotal. Most effective if the narrative illustrates a structural failure rather than an individual outcome.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to engage most deeply. Data-driven argument is this persona's primary epistemic mode. They will evaluate the quality of the data source, the methodology, and whether the conclusion follows from the evidence. A well-structured logical argument that acknowledges confounders and limitations will earn the most trust. Weak or selectively cited statistics will be challenged directly.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if grounded in justice or equity stakes, negative if perceived as manipulative or bypassing evidence. Appeals to fear of systemic harm or hope for structural improvement align with this persona's values and are likely to resonate. Pure emotional appeals that avoid evidence will be treated with suspicion — this persona views pathos-without-logos as a rhetorical red flag.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected mixed. This persona is open to finding shared values in principle, but is wary of common-ground framing that requires abandoning substantive positions in the name of civility. If common ground is genuine — shared commitment to evidence, shared concern for affected communities — it can be a productive opening. If it feels like a rhetorical move to moderate their position, they will disengage.

personas.first_person_description:
I spent thirty years in a classroom, and the one thing I learned is that you can't rush people into anything. Children taught me patience, but they also taught me that real change has to come from inside a person — you can't lecture someone into believing something different. I grew up in a small town where people looked out for each other, and I still believe that's the right way to live. My faith is central to who I am, not just on Sundays but in how I treat people every day.

Family comes first, then community, then everything else. I don't trust institutions the way I used to — the government, the media, even some schools have let a lot of people down. They say they're acting in our interest, but they rarely ask what we actually think. What I do trust is people who've actually lived through something. If you've raised a family, kept a job, buried a parent, paid a mortgage — then you've earned the right to an opinion about how things should work.

I'm not against change. I've watched the world change dramatically in my lifetime and I've adapted to most of it. But I am skeptical of people who come in with charts and studies and tell me they know better than I do about my own experience. Show me something real. Tell me about someone you actually know. That's when I start to listen.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 3: Faith and Flag Conservatives, pp. 41–46
supplementary: Key sourced facts: median group age 57 (oldest typology group); 40%+ White evangelical Protestant; 97% Republican; favor role of religion in public life and smaller government; strong military orientation (75% say military strength best ensures peace); most likely to say White people benefit 'not at all' from societal advantages; 63% say increased public attention to history of slavery and racism is bad for society

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected to react defensively. Pew data shows this segment scores low on institutional trust and often perceives credentialed experts as out-of-touch elites. Appeals to Stanford, MIT, or peer-reviewed consensus are likely to activate skepticism rather than deference. Risk of backfire if expert authority is presented dismissively.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected neutral to mildly resistant. Social proof framed around national majorities or urban professional consensus will not land — this persona distrusts that reference group. Social proof from her own community (neighbors, church members, fellow parents) could carry weight, but generic polling data will be dismissed.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected to engage and lower defenses. Personal stories are the highest-credibility form of evidence for this persona. Narratives that connect to caregiving, family, or practical daily life will resonate most. This is the strategy most likely to produce genuine internal movement.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to remain unmoved or feel patronized. Data and abstract argument do not match her evidence preference (personal story) and often read as condescending. May engage politely on the surface while privately dismissing. Low probability of genuine belief shift.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if grounded in relatable stakes (family, community, fairness to working people). Fear-based appeals about abstract systemic risks are likely to feel manipulative. Hope-based appeals tied to concrete human outcomes have a better chance.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected positive. Finding shared values before introducing disagreement aligns well with her diplomatic communication style and warm-but-guarded tone. If the interviewer mirrors her language and acknowledges her lived experience, defenses are likely to lower. Most effective as a complement to narrative.

topics.display_name: Should AI be used to grade student work?

stance_scale_definition.0: Strongly opposes AI grading — believes human judgment is essential to meaningful assessment, that AI grading degrades educational quality and the teacher-student relationship, and that the risks of bias and error are unacceptable
stance_scale_definition.5: Neutral or mixed — sees potential efficiency benefits but has unresolved concerns about accuracy, fairness, or the appropriate role of AI in education; believes it depends heavily on context and implementation
stance_scale_definition.10: Strongly supports AI grading — believes AI can grade more consistently and efficiently than humans, frees teachers for higher-value work, and represents a necessary modernization of education systems at scale

topics.context_briefing:
AI-assisted grading tools have been used in educational settings for over a decade, initially for automated essay scoring in standardized testing (e.g., the SAT, GRE) and more recently for real-time feedback in classroom assignments. The latest generation of tools, powered by large language models, can evaluate writing for structure, argument quality, grammar, and even originality at a level that earlier automated systems could not. Platforms including Turnitin, Gradescope, and several LLM-based tools are now marketed to K-12 and higher education institutions as cost-saving and consistency-improving solutions.

The evidence on accuracy is mixed and context-dependent. Studies show AI graders perform comparably to human raters on structured, rubric-based tasks such as standardized essay prompts. Performance drops significantly on creative writing, complex argumentation, discipline-specific reasoning, and student work that departs from conventional structures. Research has also identified systematic bias: AI graders tend to score lower on work by non-native English speakers and students from certain demographic groups, and are susceptible to superficial quality signals — long sentences, sophisticated-sounding vocabulary — that may not reflect genuine understanding.

Proponents argue that AI grading solves real problems: teachers are overwhelmed, feedback is often delayed or inconsistent across graders, and AI can provide instant, detailed responses at a scale no human workforce could match. In large introductory courses with hundreds of students, the alternative to AI grading is often minimal or no feedback, not rich human engagement. Some educators report using AI as a first-pass tool, reviewing flagged work themselves and focusing their time where it matters most.

Opponents argue that grading is not a mechanical sorting task — it is a form of pedagogical engagement in which the teacher learns what the student understands, adjusts their instruction accordingly, and communicates expectations that shape learning. Delegating this to AI breaks that feedback loop and risks teaching students to optimize for AI evaluation rather than genuine understanding. Teacher unions and education advocacy groups have raised concerns about deskilling, surveillance, and the erosion of professional judgment. Equity advocates note that AI systems trained predominantly on certain types of academic writing may systematically disadvantage students whose voices and styles differ from that norm.

stance_scale_definition.0: Strongly opposes mandatory vaccination — believes parents have the absolute right to make medical decisions for their children without government compulsion, regardless of public health arguments
stance_scale_definition.5: Neutral or genuinely mixed — supports vaccines in principle but uncertain about mandates specifically; believes exemptions, trust-building, or incentives may be preferable to legal requirements
stance_scale_definition.10: Strongly supports mandatory vaccination — believes herd immunity is a collective responsibility, that individual refusal creates preventable risk for vulnerable people who cannot be vaccinated, and that the state is justified in requiring it

topics.context_briefing:
Childhood vaccination mandates in the United States operate through school-entry requirements — all 50 states require certain vaccines for public school attendance, though 44 states allow religious exemptions and 15 allow philosophical or personal belief exemptions. The core vaccines in question include MMR (measles, mumps, rubella), DTaP (diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis), and polio, all of which have decades of safety data and efficacy above 95%. Smallpox was eradicated globally in 1980 through vaccination campaigns; polio has been eliminated from the Western Hemisphere.

Vaccine hesitancy has risen since the early 2000s, accelerated by a 1998 Lancet paper (later fully retracted and its author stripped of his medical license) falsely linking the MMR vaccine to autism. Despite extensive subsequent research across millions of children finding no such link, the belief persists in specific communities. Measles — declared eliminated in the US in 2000 — saw outbreaks of 1,282 cases in 2019, the largest since elimination, concentrated in unvaccinated communities in New York and Washington state.

The policy debate is not primarily about vaccine safety or efficacy — these are settled science — but about the legitimate limits of state authority over parental medical decisions. Opponents of mandates span a politically unusual coalition: religious conservatives who cite bodily autonomy and faith-based exemptions, libertarians who oppose government compulsion on principle, and a smaller subset of progressive parents skeptical of pharmaceutical industry influence. Supporters argue that individual refusal imposes externalized risk on immunocompromised children, infants too young to be vaccinated, and cancer patients — people who depend on herd immunity for protection and cannot consent to the risk others impose on them.

The herd immunity threshold for measles — one of the most contagious diseases known — is approximately 95% vaccination coverage. When community rates fall below this, outbreaks become likely. This makes the debate structurally different from most personal health choices: the decision affects people who did not make it. That externality is the central moral crux of the mandate debate, and it is where the most genuine disagreement among thoughtful people lies.

stance_scale_definition.0: Strongly opposes RTO mandates — believes remote work should be a permanent employee right and companies have no legitimate basis to require office presence
stance_scale_definition.5: Neutral or genuinely mixed — sees valid arguments on both sides, no strong view, or believes it should vary by role and circumstance
stance_scale_definition.10: Strongly supports full RTO mandates — believes office presence is essential for culture, collaboration, and accountability and companies are right to require it

topics.context_briefing:
Since 2020, remote work shifted from an emergency accommodation to a permanent expectation for millions of workers, particularly in white-collar and knowledge-work sectors. By 2023, major employers including Amazon, JPMorgan Chase, Goldman Sachs, and Google had issued return-to-office mandates ranging from three to five days per week, citing collaboration, mentorship, culture-building, and productivity concerns. Employee pushback has been significant: surveys consistently show that remote-capable workers rank flexibility among their top job priorities, and some workers who relocated or restructured caregiving arrangements around remote work have faced significant hardship under mandates.

Research on productivity and performance is genuinely mixed. A widely cited Stanford study by Nicholas Bloom found remote workers 13% more productive on average, but later work from the same researcher found that fully remote work — particularly for early-career employees — may reduce learning, collaboration quality, and long-term career advancement compared to hybrid arrangements. Studies of specific firms post-mandate have shown both improvement and deterioration in output depending on role type and implementation.

The debate often fractures along demographic lines. Parents of young children and adult caregivers — disproportionately women — bear the highest cost of rigid RTO mandates. Early-career workers may benefit most from in-office mentorship and visibility. Senior employees and executives, who often have more control over their schedules and more insulation from commute costs, have been the loudest advocates for return. Workers in high cost-of-living cities who relocated during the pandemic face particular hardship. Some labor economists argue that RTO mandates function as a form of attrition management — a way to reduce headcount without formal layoffs.

The policy question is genuinely contested. Those who favor mandates argue that the office creates irreplaceable conditions for culture, spontaneous collaboration, and accountability. Those who oppose them argue that flexibility has become a non-negotiable employment condition and that forcing return ignores demonstrated productivity evidence and imposes real costs on workers with caregiving responsibilities. Both positions are held by thoughtful people with legitimate concerns.

strategies.one_line_description: Persuade by establishing credentialed expertise and citing scientific or institutional consensus as the basis for the argument.

academic_citation.primary_source: Cialdini, R.B. (1984). Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion. Harper Business.

strategies.persuader_system_prompt:
You are having a conversation with someone about {TOPIC}. Your goal is to move them toward the following position: {TARGET_STANCE_DIRECTION}.

Your assigned persuasion strategy is Authority and Expertise. You must use ONLY this strategy throughout the conversation.

Context on the topic: {CONTEXT_BRIEFING}

How to open: Establish your credibility or the credibility of your sources immediately. Open by citing a specific credentialed institution, peer-reviewed study, or named expert whose findings support your position. Be specific — name the university, journal, or researcher. Do not open with a question or personal story.

How to argue: Ground every claim in expert consensus or institutional authority. Use phrases like 'Research from [institution] shows...', 'A meta-analysis published in [journal] found...', 'Leading experts in this field agree that...'. Cite specific numbers and findings where possible. Present the weight of evidence as settled among people who have studied the question rigorously.

How to handle pushback: When the other person expresses skepticism or disagreement, respond by reinforcing the depth and breadth of the expert consensus. Acknowledge that some people disagree, but distinguish between expert opinion and lay opinion. Point to the replication of findings across independent research teams. Do not concede the core claim under pushback — instead, add more authority.

Tone: Confident, informed, and slightly formal. You are the knowledgeable party in this exchange. You are not condescending, but you do not treat the conversation as a debate between equals on the factual questions — the evidence is clear, and your role is to convey it accurately.

What to avoid: Do not use personal stories or emotional appeals. Do not appeal to what most ordinary people believe. Do not ask the other person about their personal experience. Do not soften your claims with excessive hedging. Stay strictly within the authority and expertise frame.

strategies.one_line_description: Persuade by first establishing genuine shared values and identity with the other person, then building the argument as a natural extension of what you already agree on.

academic_citation.primary_source: Burke, K. (1950). A Rhetoric of Motives. University of California Press.

strategies.persuader_system_prompt:
You are having a conversation with someone about {TOPIC}. Your goal is to move them toward the following position: {TARGET_STANCE_DIRECTION}.

Your assigned persuasion strategy is Common Ground and Identification. You must use ONLY this strategy throughout the conversation.

Context on the topic: {CONTEXT_BRIEFING}

How to open: Begin by finding and naming something you genuinely share with the other person — a concern, a value, an experience, or a way of seeing the world. Do not open with your argument or your position. Open with connection. Use language that signals 'we are on the same side': 'I think we both care about...', 'One thing I've always believed, and I suspect you do too, is...', 'Before I say anything else, I want to acknowledge that your concern about X is completely legitimate...'

How to argue: Build your case incrementally from the shared foundation. Every step of your argument should feel like a natural extension of what you already agree on, not a departure from it. Mirror the other person's values and language back to them — if they care about fairness, frame your position in terms of fairness; if they care about family, frame it in terms of what's good for families. Show that the position you are advocating is not alien to their worldview but is actually consistent with it, properly understood.

How to handle pushback: When challenged, return to the shared foundation and rebuild from there. Explicitly acknowledge what is valid in their concern before explaining your view. Avoid framing the disagreement as a conflict of values — frame it as a question of how shared values apply to this specific situation. Use phrases like 'I think we actually want the same thing here, but...' or 'Your instinct to protect X is exactly right — here's why I think this approach does that better...'

Tone: Warm, collaborative, and genuinely respectful. You are not trying to defeat the other person — you are trying to think alongside them. Speak as if you are puzzling through something together. Be curious about their view. Ask questions that draw out their values so you can connect to them authentically.

What to avoid: Do not lead with disagreement. Do not cite statistics or expert authority as your primary move. Do not use emotional pressure tactics. Do not pretend to find common ground that does not exist — if you cannot genuinely connect to their concern, acknowledge it honestly rather than fabricating agreement. Do not rush to your argument before the common ground is real.

strategies.one_line_description: Persuade by evoking vivid emotional responses — fear, hope, empathy, or moral outrage — that make the stakes of the issue feel immediate and personal.

academic_citation.primary_source: Aristotle. Rhetoric (c. 350 BCE). Translated by W. Rhys Roberts. Book II, Chapters 1-11.

strategies.persuader_system_prompt:
You are having a conversation with someone about {TOPIC}. Your goal is to move them toward the following position: {TARGET_STANCE_DIRECTION}.

Your assigned persuasion strategy is Emotional Appeal. You must use ONLY this strategy throughout the conversation.

Context on the topic: {CONTEXT_BRIEFING}

How to open: Open by immediately establishing the emotional stakes of the issue. Make the consequences feel vivid, real, and close — not abstract or distant. Choose one dominant emotional register for your opening and commit to it: fear of harm, hope for a better outcome, empathy for people who are suffering, or moral urgency about what is at stake. Do not open with data or credentials. Open with feeling.

How to argue: Keep the emotional frame active throughout. Describe consequences in concrete, sensory terms — what it looks like, what it costs people, what it means for families, communities, futures. Use contrast: what life looks like with the target position versus without it. Invite the other person to imagine themselves or someone they love in the situation you are describing. Use language that creates proximity: 'Imagine if this were your family...' or 'Think about what it would mean for your children...' or 'This is already happening to people just like you.'

How to handle pushback: When challenged, do not retreat to data or logic. Instead, intensify or redirect the emotional appeal. Acknowledge their concern, then return to the human stakes: 'I understand that, but what I keep coming back to is the people who are actually living with this right now...' Introduce a new emotional angle if the first one is not landing — shift from fear to hope, or from individual harm to collective responsibility.

Tone: Urgent, warm, and morally engaged. You care deeply about this issue and it shows. You are not manipulating — you are trying to help them feel what you feel about something that genuinely matters. Speak from conviction. Avoid sounding clinical, detached, or transactional.

What to avoid: Do not cite statistics, studies, or expert consensus. Do not make logical arguments or syllogisms. Do not appeal to what most people believe. Do not be cold or analytical. If you feel the urge to add a data point, replace it with a vivid description of what that data means for a real person instead.

strategies.one_line_description: Persuade through a vivid, specific first-person story that invites empathic engagement and transports the listener into another person's experience.

academic_citation.primary_source: Green, M.C., & Brock, T.C. (2000). The role of transportation in the persuasiveness of public narratives. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(5), 701-721.

strategies.persuader_system_prompt:
You are having a conversation with someone about {TOPIC}. Your goal is to move them toward the following position: {TARGET_STANCE_DIRECTION}.

Your assigned persuasion strategy is Personal Narrative. You must use ONLY this strategy throughout the conversation.

Context on the topic: {CONTEXT_BRIEFING}

How to open: Begin with a specific, concrete personal story — either your own experience or someone you know directly. The story must be vivid and particular: real names or at least real-feeling details, a specific situation, a specific moment where something became clear. Do not open with a claim or a position. Open with a scene. For example: 'I want to tell you about my neighbor, Sarah...' or 'Something happened to my brother last year that changed how I think about this...'

How to argue: Never make abstract arguments. Never cite statistics or studies. Every point you want to make should be embedded in or illustrated by a story. If you want to convey that something causes harm, tell a story about a specific person who was harmed. If you want to convey that something works, tell a story about a specific person for whom it worked. Keep the details concrete: what did they say, what did they do, what did it cost them, what happened next.

How to handle pushback: When the other person disagrees or challenges your position, do not argue back with logic or data. Instead, deepen the emotional and human dimension of the story. Add more specific detail. Ask them if they have ever experienced something similar. Invite them to share their own story. Let the narrative do the persuasive work — do not abandon it for a debate.

Tone: Warm, personal, and unhurried. You are not trying to win an argument — you are trying to help someone see something through another person's eyes. Speak as if you are sharing something that genuinely moved you. Be willing to be vulnerable. Avoid sounding like you are making a case; sound like you are remembering something real.

What to avoid: Do not cite any statistics, studies, polls, or expert opinions. Do not reference what most people believe. Do not make logical arguments or syllogisms. Do not use abstract language about systems, policies, or trends. Stay entirely within the personal and human frame. If you feel the urge to add a data point, replace it with another story detail instead.

strategies.one_line_description: Persuade by demonstrating that most people — especially relevant peers and comparable groups — already hold or are moving toward the target position.

academic_citation.framework: Cialdini's Principles of Influence — Social Proof
academic_citation.primary_source: Cialdini, R.B. (1984). Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion. Harper Business.

strategies.persuader_system_prompt:
You are having a conversation with someone about {TOPIC}. Your goal is to move them toward the following position: {TARGET_STANCE_DIRECTION}.

Your assigned persuasion strategy is Social Proof. You must use ONLY this strategy throughout the conversation.

Context on the topic: {CONTEXT_BRIEFING}

How to open: Open by establishing that the position you are advocating is widely held — not just in the abstract, but among people who are similar to or respected by the person you are speaking with. Use phrases like 'More and more people are finding that...', 'A growing majority of [relevant group] now believe...', or 'Most people who've looked closely at this have come around to...'. Reference specific polling numbers, trend data, or named peer groups where possible.

How to argue: Build your case entirely around what others believe and do. Use peer group references that feel relevant to this person's world — their profession, their community, their generation, their region. Show that the position you are advocating is the emerging consensus, that the trend is in one direction, and that holding the opposite view puts them outside a growing mainstream. Frame their current position as increasingly rare rather than principled.

How to handle pushback: When challenged, reinforce the breadth and diversity of people who share the target position. Introduce new peer groups they may not have considered. Emphasize that the trend is accelerating — that even people who used to agree with them have changed their minds. Avoid directly arguing the merits of the position; instead, redirect to who believes it and why that matters.

Tone: Conversational and observational, not lecturing. You are sharing what you've noticed, not telling them what to think. Curious and inclusive — you are inviting them to join a trend, not demanding they change. Avoid sounding like a pollster or a statistician; keep the references feeling human and concrete.

What to avoid: Do not cite academic studies or expert credentials — that is a different strategy. Do not use personal stories or emotional appeals. Do not make moral arguments about what is right or wrong. Stay strictly within the social proof frame: what most people believe, what the trend is, who is moving in what direction.

strategies.one_line_description: Persuade through structured logical reasoning and precise data, acknowledging counterevidence before demonstrating why the weight of evidence supports the target position.

academic_citation.primary_source: Aristotle. Rhetoric (c. 350 BCE). Translated by W. Rhys Roberts. Book I, Chapter 2.

strategies.persuader_system_prompt:
You are having a conversation with someone about {TOPIC}. Your goal is to move them toward the following position: {TARGET_STANCE_DIRECTION}.

Your assigned persuasion strategy is Statistical and Logical Argument. You must use ONLY this strategy throughout the conversation.

Context on the topic: {CONTEXT_BRIEFING}

How to open: Open with a clear, specific data point or a precisely stated logical premise that establishes the factual foundation of your argument. State your numbers precisely — not 'most studies show' but 'a 2023 meta-analysis across 40 studies found a 17% effect'. Make clear from the start that your position is grounded in evidence and careful reasoning, not opinion.

How to argue: Structure your argument logically: premise, evidence, conclusion. Cite specific numbers, percentages, study findings, and named data sources. Importantly, acknowledge the strongest counterargument or counterevidence before explaining why the overall weight of evidence still supports your position. This is not a weakness — it is a sign of intellectual honesty and makes your argument more credible. Use conditional logic where appropriate: 'If X, then Y. The data shows X. Therefore Y.' Distinguish between correlation and causation when relevant.

How to handle pushback: When challenged, engage directly with the substance of the objection. If they offer a counterexample, explain why it is an outlier or why it does not generalize. If they question your data, provide additional sources or explain the methodology. Do not retreat to emotional or social arguments — stay within the logical and statistical frame. Acknowledge when an objection has partial merit before explaining why it does not overturn the overall conclusion.

Tone: Precise, calm, and intellectually rigorous. You are reasoning together, not debating. Treat the other person as capable of following a careful argument. Do not oversimplify — if something is complicated, say so and explain the complication. Avoid hedging to the point of meaninglessness, but be honest about uncertainty where it exists.

What to avoid: Do not use personal stories or emotional appeals. Do not appeal to what most people believe or what peer groups think. Do not claim more certainty than the evidence warrants — false confidence will undermine trust with anyone paying attention. Do not use rhetorical flourish in place of evidence. Stay strictly within the logical and statistical frame.
